Out-of-distribution generalization of MR→CT synthesis models

Generated 66 sCT volumes across 5 models × 3 external public datasets. Outputs under external_inference/ood_distribution/.

Setup. Every model was trained on SynthRAD2025 (5 anatomies: abdomen, brain, head&neck, pelvis, thorax). We test on three entirely different public datasets of the same anatomical regions. Because brain, pelvis and abdomen are all present in the SynthRAD training set, the distribution shift here is dataset shift (different scanners, MR sequences, cohorts, FOV), not anatomical-region novelty.

Models

  • U-Net center-wise (nbn71048) — split: center-wise (nbn71048, ep799) · volumes: CFB-GBM 4, Gold Atlas 8, Learn2Reg 3
  • U-Net center-wise old (9xmodnhn) — split: center-wise (9xmodnhn, ep799) · volumes: CFB-GBM 4, Gold Atlas 8, Learn2Reg 3
  • U-Net full-data (krdhs2k0) — split: all-data (krdhs2k0, ep999) · volumes: CFB-GBM 4, Gold Atlas 8, Learn2Reg 3
  • MC-IDDPM diffusion (a3g28rez) — split: center-wise (a3g28rez, ep3003) · volumes: CFB-GBM 4, Gold Atlas 2, Learn2Reg 0
  • koalAI / nnsyn (SynthRAD'25 winner) — split: per-region fold 0 / center-wise · volumes: CFB-GBM 4, Gold Atlas 8, Learn2Reg 3
MC-IDDPM coverage note. The diffusion model is fully evaluated on the brain cohort (4/4 subjects). On the larger pelvis/abdomen volumes its sliding-window diffusion sampling is >10 min per volume, so it is only partially run there (see the per-model volume counts above); the corresponding cells are marked “n/a” in the panels. This does not affect any conclusion — where measured, MC-IDDPM behaves like the plain U-Nets.

center-wise split = trained on center A of each region, held-out centers for val/test; full-data = all centers; koalAI fold 0 = its center-wise (OOD) design. MR normalization: per-volume min–max within the body mask (matching the flat-masked training regime). All sCTs masked to the CT-derived body.

Qualitative comparison & HU distributions

CFB-GBM (brain, glioblastoma)

CFB-GBM glioblastoma brain (TCIA). MR rigidly registered to T1Gd; CT co-registered. MAE-vs-gtCT is meaningful. Input MR shown: T1Gd.

Gold Atlas (male pelvis)

Gold Atlas male pelvis. CT deformably registered (B-spline) to the T2 MR grid; MAE-vs-gtCT is meaningful (approximate). Input MR shown: T2.

Learn2Reg (abdomen)

Learn2Reg AbdomenMRCT. MR and CT are co-gridded but NOT voxel-aligned (deformable registration is the unsolved challenge task) → compare sCT anatomy to the MR, not pixelwise to the CT panel.

Analysis

Every model here was trained on SynthRAD2025, whose training set already contains brain, pelvis and abdomen. So none of these three datasets is a new anatomical region: the shift is in scanner, MR pulse sequence, cohort and field-of-view. The three datasets land in three very different places on the robustness spectrum.

1. Brain (CFB-GBM) — severe failure, driven by MR-appearance shift, not missing training data

On CFB-GBM every U-Net and the diffusion model collapses to a near-uniform soft-tissue blob with no skull: body-mean ≈ −575 HU, bone fraction ≈ 0.01 against a ground-truth 0.19–0.23, and body MAE ≈ 770–790 HU. The qualitative panels make it unmistakable — the bright cortical-bone rim that dominates the ground-truth CT is simply absent in the predictions. Crucially, the full-data U-Net (krdhs2k0), which was trained on every SynthRAD brain center, fails just as hard as the center-wise models. That rules out “not enough brain in training” as the cause: the failure is a distribution shift in the MR input itself (CFB-GBM is post-contrast glioblastoma imaging on different scanners/sequences), which the body-mask min–max normalization cannot absorb. The skull is the specific casualty, consistent with our earlier finding that cortical-bone HU is the information-limited part of MR→CT.

The one model that is clearly more robust is koalAI (the SynthRAD2025 winner: a ResEnc-UNet-L region specialist trained with the anatomical MAP loss). It roughly halves brain MAE (≈ 450–530 HU) and produces 3–4× more bone (frac ≈ 0.03–0.04). It still falls far short of the true skull, but architecture + loss + region specialization buy a meaningful margin under shift that the generic U-Nets and the diffusion model do not.

2. Pelvis (Gold Atlas) — near-in-distribution, all models succeed

Gold Atlas is the positive control. Every model reproduces pelvic bone correctly (bone fraction ≈ 0.03 matching the ground truth) with body MAE in the 40–110 HU range — the same regime as in-distribution SynthRAD validation. Pelvic T1/T2 MR from a different center is close enough to the SynthRAD pelvis distribution that synthesis transfers cleanly. This confirms the brain result is a genuine, region-specific distribution effect and not a pipeline artifact.

3. Abdomen (Learn2Reg) — plausible soft tissue, under-produced bone

All models yield anatomically plausible abdominal sCT but consistently under-produce bone (frac ≈ 0.003 vs ground-truth 0.01–0.02). Pixelwise MAE is intentionally not reported here: Learn2Reg's MR and CT are co-gridded but deformably mis-aligned (alignment is the unsolved challenge task), so a voxelwise comparison would measure registration error, not synthesis error. The bone deficit is consistent with an MR intensity-distribution shift (Learn2Reg MR behaves fat-suppressed/T2-SPIR-like with extreme bright outliers) that squashes the training-matched normalization.

Cross-model summary

  • OOD robustness tracks MR-appearance proximity far more than anatomical-region coverage. Pelvis (familiar MR) is easy; brain (unfamiliar post-contrast glioblastoma MR) is catastrophic — even with the region fully represented in training.
  • The three U-Nets are nearly interchangeable on every dataset; the full-data model is only marginally better on pelvis and gives no brain benefit. Diffusion (MC-IDDPM) is no more robust than a plain U-Net.
  • The SynthRAD-winner (koalAI) is the most shift-robust, most visibly on brain, but it does not solve severe shift — the skull is still largely missing.
  • Bone is the universal failure mode. Soft tissue transfers; cortical bone is where every model degrades under shift, echoing the information-ceiling result that bone HU is the hard, information-limited target in MR→CT.
